papers.full_text: Abstract The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus (ANDV) renewed concerns regarding the pandemic potential of hantaviruses, particularly because ANDV is the only hantavirus with proved human-to-human transmission. These concerns and fears are justified by the profound negative impact of the recent COVID-19 pandemic. Hantaviruses are segmented negative-sense RNA viruses maintained mainly in rodents and transmitted to man primarily through inhalation of contaminated aerosols. Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome predominates in Europe and Asia, whereas hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome occurs in North and South Americas and carries high mortality. Although most hantaviruses have no human-to-human transmissibility, ANDV outbreaks demonstrate that transmission between humans is possible under close-contact conditions. Comparison with previous pandemics and WHO priority pathogens highlights several pandemic-associated features shared by hantaviruses, including zoonotic origin, RNA genome variability, and environmental influences. In this review, we will try to find the answer for the question “Can hantaviruses cause the next pandemic?”. Similar content being viewed by others Introduction On May 2, 2026, a deadly outbreak of hantavirus infection on the cruise ship MV Hondius in the Atlantic Ocean was reported. At that time, according to the ship operator, 147 passengers and crew members remained onboard, while 34 individuals had previously disembarked. By 13 May 2026, the ongoing outbreak had resulted in a total of 11 confirmed cases and three deaths, corresponding to a case fatality rate of approximately 27%.The early reported cases were laboratory-confirmed as Andes virus (ANDV) infections [1]. ANDV is a member of the family Hantaviridae .Other members of the family are Hantaan virus (HTNV), Seoul virus (SEOV), Dobrava-Belgrade virus (DOBV), Puumala virus (PUUV) and Sin Nombre virus (SNV) and all are highly pathogenic to humans [2]. News and reports about this outbreak drew attention of the public as well as the scientific community and an important question emerged, Can this outbreak on the cruise lead to a pandemic? In this article, we will review the current knowledge on hantaviruses, and lessons from the past pandemics, trying to find the answer for this important question (Fig. 1). May 13 : 11 cases, including 3 deaths. - A: April 1, Argentina:Departure with no reported symptomatic passengers. - B: At sea, April 6 :Index case symptoms, 70 year old Dutch man , April 11 died from respiratory distress. - C: St.Helena, April 24 :23 passengers disembark to return to their countries.The wife of the index case (now the 2nd case) disembark to Johannesburg. - D: Johannesburg, April 27, 2nd case died, 3rd case confirmed. - E: At sea, May 2, 4th case died on board. - F: At sea, May 4, two new cases suspected. - G: Switzerland, May 4, one confirmed case. - H: Netherlands, May 6, two confirmed cases. - I: France, May 10, one confirmed case. - J: United sataes, May 10, one confirmed case. History of hantaviruses During the Korean Peninsula war (from 1950 till1953), about three thousands of the soldier of the United Nations Command developed hemorrhagic fever and features of acute kidney injury, that was then described as hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS). The definite etiology of this disease was not discovered until 1981, when Lee et al. described HTNV, and its presence in the lungs of the striped field mouse (Apodemus agrarius). Nephropathia epidemica (NE), a condition resembling HFRS but less severe, was described in Fennoscandia since the early 1930s, but its causative virus, the PUUV was discovered in rodents bank voles in the year 1980 in Finland [2]. In 1993, an outbreak of a severe pulmonary condition occurred in the Four Corners region of the USA, with a rapidly progressive course resulting in adult respiratory distress syndrome.This was discovered to be a result of infection with a new hantavirus the SNV and the disease was termed hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS) [3]. This was the first description of this syndrome and the first identification of hantavirus diseases in the Americas. In subsequent years, additional hantaviruses responsible for HCPS were identified throughout the Americas, with ANDV being recognized in 1995. The first documented man-to-man transmission of ANDV occurred during an outbreak in El Bolsón, Argentina, in 1996. Since that event, several sporadic outbreaks involving interpersonal transmission have also been described [4]. Currently, more than 28 hantavirus species pathogenic to humans have been identified globally, causing a broad clinical spectrum ranging from acute kidney injury and pulmonary edema to severe hemorrhagic manifestations. Although approximately 1,000 HCPS cases have been documented, the annual global burden of HFRS is estimated to exceed 100,000 cases. Because hantavirus infections are frequently underdiagnosed and underreported in many regions, additional unidentified hantaviruses may still exist [3]. Morphological characteristics and genomic structure According to the international committee of taxonomy of viruses, the hantaviruses now belong to the family Hantaviridae of the Class Bunyaviricetes [2]. The virion of hantaviruses is spherical with a diameter of 120–160 nm (Fig. 2).The viral envelop is composed of a 5 nm thickness lipid bilayer containing embedded glycoprotein surface spikes extending to 10 nm from the lipid bilayer [5]. Similar to most of the members of the Class Bunyaviricetes, the hantavirus genome consists of three single-stranded, negative-sense RNA segments designated as large (L), medium (M), and small (S). The L segment encodes the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which is essential for viral RNA transcription and genome replication. The M segment produces a glycoprotein precursor that undergoes cleavage by host-cell signal peptidases to generate the surface glycoproteins Gn and Gc. These glycoproteins mediate attachment to host-cell receptors and play a major role in triggering immune activation and antibody production. The S segment encodes the nucleocapsid (N) protein, which play a role in stabilization of viral RNA. In some hantaviruses, including ANDV, PUUV, SNV, and Tula virus, the S genomic segment encodes a nonstructural protein capable of suppressing interferon production within host cells [7]. Cell entery and viral replication Hantavirus infection begins with the attachment of viral glycoprotein surface spikes to specific receptors on host cell membranes (Fig. 3). In certain hantaviruses, cell entry may also involve integrins, which are transmembrane heterodimeric glycoproteins. After receptor binding, the virus enters host cells through endocytic pathways or macropinocytosis, which may be clathrin-, caveolin-, or cholesterol-dependent. For example, HTNV and SEOV primarily utilize clathrin-mediated endocytosis, whereas ANDV can enter cells via cholesterol-dependent macropinocytosis [8]. After entering the host cell, virions become enclosed within clathrin-coated vesicles originating from specialized clathrin-coated areas of the plasma membrane [9]. The particles are then trafficked sequentially to early endosomes and subsequently to late endosomal and lysosomal compartments. Fusion of the viral envelope with the endosomal membrane enables the release of viral ribonucleoprotein complexes into the cytoplasm, where RdRp mediates viral genome replication and transcription. Messenger RNAs transcribed from the L and S segments are translated by free ribosomes in the cytoplasm, while those derived from the M segment are translated on membrane-bound ribosomes associated with the endoplasmic reticulum and rough endoplasmic reticulum. Newly synthesized virions are assembled within the Golgi apparatus and are thereafter transported to the cell surface for release via exocytosis [10]. Transmission, ecology and epidemiology Although hantaviruses are susceptible to inactivation by heating at 60 °C for 30 min, exposure to ultraviolet light, and treatment with organic or hypochlorite-based solvents [3], they remain relatively stable in environmental conditions. The viruses are capable of surviving for approximately 10 days at room temperature and for about 18 days at lower temperatures such as 4 °C [11], which contributes significantly to their potential transmission. In contrast to many other bunyaviruses, which are spread by insect vectors, hantaviruses infecting humans are primarily rodent-borne [12]. Most hantavirus infections in rodents are asymptomatic and persistent. An exception is infection of Syrian hamsters with ANDV, which can produce clinical manifestations resembling HCPS [13]. Several small mammals, including rodents, moles and shrews act as natural reservoirs for distinct hantavirus species and are capable of transmitting the virus through both horizontal and vertical routes (Fig. 4). Transmission among rodents occurs through droplet or aerosols generated from contaminated secretions and excreta such as urine, feces, and saliva, as well as contaminated food, biting, and scratching [13]. Fecal–oral transmission has also been documented, and infected females may pass the virus transplacentally to their offspring [6]. Behaviors such as mating and fighting further facilitate viral spread among susceptible animals. Humans typically acquire hantavirus infection through inhalation of aerosolized particles contaminated with rodent excreta or through ingestion of contaminated food [14]. Bites or scratches also represent possible routes of human transmission [6]. ANDV is currently the only hantavirus known to spread directly from person to person, usually after close contact with infected individuals .The virus may be present in saliva and transmitted through respiratory droplets generated by coughing or sneezing [15]. Evidence also suggests that ANDV can be transmitted via breast milk, as well as through placental and digestive routes [16]. Additional studies indicate that mites may contribute to the transmission of HFRS to both animals and humans [17]. Pigs have also been shown to acquire HTNV infection without developing severe disease [18]. Antibodies against HTNV and PUUV have additionally been identified in other animals like rabbits and cattle, while SNV antibodies have been detected in dogs and cats [19]. Nevertheless, the ecological significance of these animal hosts in hantavirus transmission remains unclear. The geographic patterns of human hantavirus infections closely correspond to the distribution of their rodent reservoir hosts. Consequently, HFRS is predominantly reported in Europe and Asia, caused by the so-called “old world hantaviruses”, whereas HCPS has been documented exclusively in the Americas, with specific hantavirus species, the so-called “new world hantaviruses” occurring in regions where their natural hosts are prevalent [20]. Currently, the annual global incidence of HFRS is estimated at approximately 100,000 cases. China remains the most highly endemic country, contributing nearly 70–90% of all reported HFRS cases, with about 40,000–60,000 cases recorded annually in recent years [21]. Nevertheless, the actual burden of infection is likely underestimated, as asymptomatic or mildly symptomatic cases probably exceed the number of clinically apparent infections. Furthermore, hantavirus infection is not classified as a notifiable disease in all countries where human cases have been identified [22]. Epidemiology of old world hantaviruses To date, seven hantavirus species have been linked to HFRS .In Asia, the most severe forms of the disease are mainly attributed to HTNV and Amur/Soochong virus infections, with case fatality of approximately 15%. By contrast, SEOV infection is generally associated with less severe clinical course and a lower case fatality rate of about 1–2%. Each year, an estimated 40,000–60,000 HFRS cases are documented, with nearly 99% reported from China [3]. Across Europe, more than 9,000 HFRS cases are reported annually, the majority resulting from PUUV infection. PUUV is widely distributed throughout northern and central Europe, the Balkan Peninsula, and parts of Russia, corresponding to the habitat of its rodent reservoir, Myodes glareolus. Infection with PUUV typically produces the milder form NE. The highest numbers of PUUV-related cases have been recorded in Finland, Sweden, and regions of European Russia. During recent years, reports of NE cases are increasing in Germany and Belgium, with additional cases reported in Norway, France, Austria, Hungary, Slovenia, and neighboring countries [23]. In Europe, severe HFRS cases are mainly caused by DOBV carried by Apodemus flavicollis. Although the geographic distribution of this rodent host extends beyond southeastern Europe, severe DOBV-associated infections have thus far been recognized primarily within the Balkan region [24]. Another hantavirus lineage, SAAV (DOBV-Aa), which is associated with Apodemus agrarius, has been identified as the cause of a comparatively mild form of HFRS in countries including Russia and Slovakia .Unlike DOBV-associated HFRS in the Balkans, where mortality rates may approach 12% ,fatal outcomes have not been reported in SAAV-related infections. More recently, an additional DOBV lineage was detected in Apodemus ponticus rodents from the Sochi region of European Russia and was linked to intermediate severity of HFRS manifestations. The variation in disease severity among the genetically related DOBV lineages may partly reflect differences in their respective rodent reservoir hosts [6]. Tula virus has also been implicated in human disease in Germany, Switzerland, and the Czech Republic; however, definitive evidence confirming its pathogenic role in humans remains limited [6]. Serological investigations have further identified evidence of human hantavirus exposure in countries such as Italy, Latvia, Lithuania, Moldova, Spain, and the United Kingdom .Nevertheless, confirmed clinical cases have not yet been formally documented in these regions [25]. Epidemiology of new world hantaviruses HCPS was first identified as a hantavirus-associated disease in 1993 in the Four Corners region of the United States. Since that initial outbreak, confirmed HCPS cases have also been documented in Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Canada, Chile, Panama, Paraguay, and Uruguay. At least 15 hantavirus species have been associated with HCPS in the Americas. Approximately 200 cases are reported annually across North and South America combined. Although the incidence of HCPS is considerably lower than that of HFRS, the syndrome is associated with a substantially higher mortality rate, averaging nearly 40%. SNV represents the principal etiologic agent of HCPS in the United States, whereas ANDV is the predominant causative virus in South America [20]. Importantly, ANDV remains the only hantavirus conclusively shown to undergo person-to-person transmission, and infections are associated with high fatality rates [26]. Similar to HFRS-associated hantaviruses, HCPS-related viruses may also produce asymptomatic or subclinical infections that remain unrecognized. Supporting this observation, several seroepidemiological studies have demonstrated relatively high antibody prevalence in certain populations despite the low number of clinically diagnosed HCPS cases [25]. Hantavirus infection in Africa The first serological indications of hantavirus infection in Africa were documented in 1984, when Gonzalez et al. detected hantavirus antibodies in rodents and humans from the Central African Republic, Gabon, Burkina Faso, and Benin [27]. Later investigations also provided serological evidence of human exposure in several other African countries, including Guinea, Senegal, Nigeria, Djibouti, and Egypt [25]. Nevertheless, despite these seroepidemiological findings, the literature has reported only a single suspected African case of HFRS to date [28] Pathogenesis of hantaviruses The severity of hantavirus infection in humans differs according to the viral species and strain involved [14]. HFRS predominantly involves the kidneys and vascular system, HCPS mainly targets the lungs and heart. Additional organs, including the liver, spleen, and nervous system, may also be affected. In both humans and animals, hantaviruses primarily infect the endothelial cells in the lung or the kidney as well as macrophages [29]. The principal pathological features of the described hantavirus diseases include increased vascular permeability and acute decrease of the platelet count [3]. Infection is initiated through binding of the viral glycoproteins Gn and Gc to β-integrin receptors, such as αvβ1 and αvβ3, located on the membranes of target cells [29]. Immature dendritic cells situated near endothelial cells transport virions from lymphatic vessels to regional lymph nodes, facilitating further infection of endothelial cells. These dendritic cells also function as antigen-presenting cells, triggering immune responses involving macrophages and CD8 + T lymphocytes [3]. A delayed type I interferon response contributes to elevated viral loads. Following immune activation, cytotoxic T cells release pro-inflammatory cytokines that damage infected endothelial cells, thereby increasing vascular permeability and promoting inflammation [29]. In addition, complement activation and secretion of inflammatory mediators—including interferons, interleukins such as IL-1, IL-6, and IL-10, and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) have major roles in altering endothelial barrier function. The nature and strenghth of the immune response vary among different hantavirus species.Patients with HFRS commonly exhibit increased levels of CD8 + T lymphocytes. Elevated concentrations of IL-6 have been linked to thrombocytopenia and impaired renal function [30]. Human leukocyte antigen (HLA) molecules play an essential role in presenting vira…

full_text_sections.full_text: Abstract The 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus (ANDV) renewed concerns regarding the pandemic potential of hantaviruses, particularly because ANDV is the only hantavirus with proved human-to-human transmission. These concerns and fears are justified by the profound negative impact of the recent COVID-19 pandemic. Hantaviruses are segmented negative-sense RNA viruses maintained mainly in rodents and transmitted to man primarily through inhalation of contaminated aerosols. Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome predominates in Europe and Asia, whereas hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome occurs in North and South Americas and carries high mortality. Although most hantaviruses have no human-to-human transmissibility, ANDV outbreaks demonstrate that transmission between humans is possible under close-contact conditions. Comparison with previous pandemics and WHO priority pathogens highlights several pandemic-associated features shared by hantaviruses, including zoonotic origin, RNA genome variability, and environmental influences. In this review, we will try to find the answer for the question “Can hantaviruses cause the next pandemic?”. Similar content being viewed by others Introduction On May 2, 2026, a deadly outbreak of hantavirus infection on the cruise ship MV Hondius in the Atlantic Ocean was reported. At that time, according to the ship operator, 147 passengers and crew members remained onboard, while 34 individuals had previously disembarked. By 13 May 2026, the ongoing outbreak had resulted in a total of 11 confirmed cases and three deaths, corresponding to a case fatality rate of approximately 27%.The early reported cases were laboratory-confirmed as Andes virus (ANDV) infections [1]. ANDV is a member of the family Hantaviridae .Other members of the family are Hantaan virus (HTNV), Seoul virus (SEOV), Dobrava-Belgrade virus (DOBV), Puumala virus (PUUV) and Sin Nombre virus (SNV) and all are highly pathogenic to humans [2]. News and reports about this outbreak drew attention of the public as well as the scientific community and an important question emerged, Can this outbreak on the cruise lead to a pandemic? In this article, we will review the current knowledge on hantaviruses, and lessons from the past pandemics, trying to find the answer for this important question (Fig. 1). May 13 : 11 cases, including 3 deaths. - A: April 1, Argentina:Departure with no reported symptomatic passengers. - B: At sea, April 6 :Index case symptoms, 70 year old Dutch man , April 11 died from respiratory distress. - C: St.Helena, April 24 :23 passengers disembark to return to their countries.The wife of the index case (now the 2nd case) disembark to Johannesburg. - D: Johannesburg, April 27, 2nd case died, 3rd case confirmed. - E: At sea, May 2, 4th case died on board. - F: At sea, May 4, two new cases suspected. - G: Switzerland, May 4, one confirmed case. - H: Netherlands, May 6, two confirmed cases. - I: France, May 10, one confirmed case. - J: United sataes, May 10, one confirmed case. History of hantaviruses During the Korean Peninsula war (from 1950 till1953), about three thousands of the soldier of the United Nations Command developed hemorrhagic fever and features of acute kidney injury, that was then described as hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS). The definite etiology of this disease was not discovered until 1981, when Lee et al. described HTNV, and its presence in the lungs of the striped field mouse (Apodemus agrarius). Nephropathia epidemica (NE), a condition resembling HFRS but less severe, was described in Fennoscandia since the early 1930s, but its causative virus, the PUUV was discovered in rodents bank voles in the year 1980 in Finland [2]. In 1993, an outbreak of a severe pulmonary condition occurred in the Four Corners region of the USA, with a rapidly progressive course resulting in adult respiratory distress syndrome.This was discovered to be a result of infection with a new hantavirus the SNV and the disease was termed hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS) [3]. This was the first description of this syndrome and the first identification of hantavirus diseases in the Americas. In subsequent years, additional hantaviruses responsible for HCPS were identified throughout the Americas, with ANDV being recognized in 1995. The first documented man-to-man transmission of ANDV occurred during an outbreak in El Bolsón, Argentina, in 1996. Since that event, several sporadic outbreaks involving interpersonal transmission have also been described [4]. Currently, more than 28 hantavirus species pathogenic to humans have been identified globally, causing a broad clinical spectrum ranging from acute kidney injury and pulmonary edema to severe hemorrhagic manifestations. Although approximately 1,000 HCPS cases have been documented, the annual global burden of HFRS is estimated to exceed 100,000 cases. Because hantavirus infections are frequently underdiagnosed and underreported in many regions, additional unidentified hantaviruses may still exist [3]. Morphological characteristics and genomic structure According to the international committee of taxonomy of viruses, the hantaviruses now belong to the family Hantaviridae of the Class Bunyaviricetes [2]. The virion of hantaviruses is spherical with a diameter of 120–160 nm (Fig. 2).The viral envelop is composed of a 5 nm thickness lipid bilayer containing embedded glycoprotein surface spikes extending to 10 nm from the lipid bilayer [5]. Similar to most of the members of the Class Bunyaviricetes, the hantavirus genome consists of three single-stranded, negative-sense RNA segments designated as large (L), medium (M), and small (S). The L segment encodes the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which is essential for viral RNA transcription and genome replication. The M segment produces a glycoprotein precursor that undergoes cleavage by host-cell signal peptidases to generate the surface glycoproteins Gn and Gc. These glycoproteins mediate attachment to host-cell receptors and play a major role in triggering immune activation and antibody production. The S segment encodes the nucleocapsid (N) protein, which play a role in stabilization of viral RNA. In some hantaviruses, including ANDV, PUUV, SNV, and Tula virus, the S genomic segment encodes a nonstructural protein capable of suppressing interferon production within host cells [7]. Cell entery and viral replication Hantavirus infection begins with the attachment of viral glycoprotein surface spikes to specific receptors on host cell membranes (Fig. 3). In certain hantaviruses, cell entry may also involve integrins, which are transmembrane heterodimeric glycoproteins. After receptor binding, the virus enters host cells through endocytic pathways or macropinocytosis, which may be clathrin-, caveolin-, or cholesterol-dependent. For example, HTNV and SEOV primarily utilize clathrin-mediated endocytosis, whereas ANDV can enter cells via cholesterol-dependent macropinocytosis [8]. After entering the host cell, virions become enclosed within clathrin-coated vesicles originating from specialized clathrin-coated areas of the plasma membrane [9]. The particles are then trafficked sequentially to early endosomes and subsequently to late endosomal and lysosomal compartments. Fusion of the viral envelope with the endosomal membrane enables the release of viral ribonucleoprotein complexes into the cytoplasm, where RdRp mediates viral genome replication and transcription. Messenger RNAs transcribed from the L and S segments are translated by free ribosomes in the cytoplasm, while those derived from the M segment are translated on membrane-bound ribosomes associated with the endoplasmic reticulum and rough endoplasmic reticulum. Newly synthesized virions are assembled within the Golgi apparatus and are thereafter transported to the cell surface for release via exocytosis [10]. Transmission, ecology and epidemiology Although hantaviruses are susceptibl…

analysis.background: The paper discusses the 2026 MV Hondius outbreak of Andes virus (ANDV) and raises concerns about the pandemic potential of hantaviruses, especially because ANDV is the only hantavirus with proven human‑to‑human transmission (A1). The concern is amplified by the recent impact of the COVID‑19 pandemic (A2).
analysis.methods: Not reported in the supplied evidence.
analysis.results: The outbreak involved 147 persons on board, with 11 laboratory‑confirmed ANDV cases and three deaths, giving a case‑fatality rate of ~27% (F9‑F10, F14). ANDV is the sole hantavirus shown to transmit between humans under close contact (A5). Most hantaviruses lack such transmissibility, but they share pandemic‑associated traits such as zoonotic origin, RNA genome variability, and environmental influences (A6).
analysis.contribution: The review highlights features that could enable hantaviruses to become pandemic threats, informing risk assessment, surveillance priorities, and public‑health preparedness (A6).
analysis.limitations: The article is a narrative review with limited primary epidemiological data and no detailed methods; conclusions rely on a single outbreak description and literature synthesis.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The 2026 MV Hondius Andes virus outbreak raised concerns about hantavirus pandemic potential, especially since ANDV uniquely shows human‑to‑human transmission (A1, A2). Methods: Not reported in the supplied evidence. Results: Among 147 onboard individuals, 11 confirmed cases and three deaths yielded a ~27% case‑fatality rate (F9‑F10, F14); ANDV can transmit between humans, unlike most hantaviruses, yet shares zoonotic, RNA‑virus variability, and environmental traits linked to pandemics (A5, A6). Contribution: The review informs risk assessment and surveillance by outlining pandemic‑relevant characteristics of hantaviruses (A6). Limitations: It is a narrative review lacking systematic methods and primary data, relying on a single outbreak report.

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒 会引起流行病吗?

analysis_zh.background: 本文讨论了2026年MV Hondius的安第斯病毒(ANDV)暴发,并对汉坦病毒的流行病潜力提出担忧,尤其因为ANDV是唯一已证实有人际传播的汉坦病毒(A1)。近期COVID-19大流行的影响进一步加剧了这种担忧(A2)。
analysis_zh.methods: 所提供的证据中未报告。
analysis_zh.results: 此次暴发涉及机上147人,其中11例实验室确诊的ANDV病例,导致3例死亡,病例致死率约为27%(F9‐F10,F14)。ANDV是唯一在密切接触下证实可在人际间传播的汉坦病毒(A5)。大多数汉坦病毒缺乏此类传播能力,但它们共享诸如动物源起源、RNA基因组变异性和环境因素等与大流行相关的特征(A6)。
analysis_zh.contribution: 本综述强调了可能使汉坦病毒成为大流行威胁的特征,为风险评估、监测重点和公共卫生准备提供参考(A6)。
analysis_zh.limitations: 本文为叙述性综述,原始流行病学数据有限且缺乏详细方法;结论依赖于单一暴发的描述和文献综合。

papers.title: Hantavirus Diseases: A Review of Virology, Clinical Features, and Therapeutic Approaches
papers.abstract: Hantaviruses are worldwide zoonotic infections that cause two main human diseases: Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome (HFRS) in Eurasia and Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome (HPS) in the Americas . These diseases are acquired mostly by inhaling aerosolized excreta from infected rodent reservoirs but transmission may also occur by direct contact or, rarely, person-to-person transmission with some South American strains. Hantaviruses produce severe morbidity and mortality, the severity of which depends on the viral species, the host immunological response, and the timeliness of medical assistance. Much has been learned over the last several decades from large-scale studies on the distribution of reservoir hosts, environmental determinants of virus persistence, and spillover into human populations that provide essential insight into hantavirus ecology. Significant progress has also been achieved in understanding molecular virology and immunopathogenesis, revealing that much of the clinical severity is due to immune-mediated vascular leakage rather than direct viral cytopathic effects. Yet despite the scientific improvements, there are still big gaps. There are no licensed vaccines for global use and antiviral treatments are limited in scope and efficacy with supportive care being the mainstay of clinical management . Diagnostic methods are improving, but species-specific identification is problematic in locations where numerous hantaviruses cocirculate. Furthermore, environmental change, urbanization, and changing rodent population dynamics are providing more opportunities for viral development and global spread. This review covers the present state of knowledge about hantavirus biology, illness features, clinical management, diagnostic methods, prevention efforts, and research objectives to reduce the global burden of hantavirus disease.
authors: Chetna Baregama1, Suresh Kumar Dev1, Vijay Singh Kachawa2, Ayush Garg1, Mohammad Junaid Alam Mansoori1, Yogesh Kumar Apurva1, Vijay Kumar Bansal3, Vaibhav Rathore*4, Akhil Mangal5
papers.journal: Zenodo (CERN European Organization for Nuclear Research)

source_records.title: Hantavirus Diseases: A Review of Virology, Clinical Features, and Therapeutic Approaches
source_records.abstract: Hantaviruses are worldwide zoonotic infections that cause two main human diseases: Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome (HFRS) in Eurasia and Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome (HPS) in the Americas . These diseases are acquired mostly by inhaling aerosolized excreta from infected rodent reservoirs but transmission may also occur by direct contact or, rarely, person-to-person transmission with some South American strains. Hantaviruses produce severe morbidity and mortality, the severity of which depends on the viral species, the host immunological response, and the timeliness of medical assistance. Much has been learned over the last several decades from large-scale studies on the distribution of reservoir hosts, environmental determinants of virus persistence, and spillover into human populations that provide essential insight into hantavirus ecology. Significant progress has also been achieved in understanding molecular virology and immunopathogenesis, revealing that much of the clinical severity is due to immune-mediated vascular leakage rather than direct viral cytopathic effects. Yet despite the scientific improvements, there are still big gaps. There are no licensed vaccines for global use and antiviral treatments are limited in scope and efficacy with supportive care being the mainstay of clinical management . Diagnostic methods are improving, but species-specific identification is problematic in locations where numerous hantaviruses cocirculate. Furthermore, environmental change, urbanization, and changing rodent population dynamics are providing more opportunities for viral development and global spread. This review covers the present state of knowledge about hantavirus biology, illness features, clinical management, diagnostic methods, prevention efforts, and research objectives to reduce the global burden of hantavirus disease.
authors: Chetna Baregama1, Suresh Kumar Dev1, Vijay Singh Kachawa2, Ayush Garg1, Mohammad Junaid Alam Mansoori1, Yogesh Kumar Apurva1, Vijay Kumar Bansal3, Vaibhav Rathore*4, Akhil Mangal5

analysis.background: Hantaviruses are worldwide zoonotic agents that cause Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome in Eurasia and Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome in the Americas, motivating a comprehensive review.
analysis.main_directions: The review addresses rodent‑borne transmission, immune‑mediated vascular leakage pathogenesis, limited therapeutic options, diagnostic challenges, and the impact of environmental change on spread.
analysis.current_state: Disease severity depends on viral species, host immune response, and prompt medical care, with immune‑driven vascular leakage identified as the primary driver of severe outcomes.
analysis.gaps: There are no globally licensed vaccines, antiviral treatments are scarce, species‑specific diagnostics are difficult, and environmental and urbanization trends heighten spillover risk.
analysis.future_research: Priorities include large‑scale ecological surveillance of reservoir hosts, investigation of environmental determinants, and development of vaccines and targeted antivirals.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantaviruses cause HFRS and HPS worldwide, prompting review. Main directions: transmission, immune‑mediated pathogenesis, limited therapies, diagnostic issues, and environmental drivers are discussed. Current state: severity varies by virus, host immunity, and care timing, with vascular leakage as key pathology. Gaps: no global vaccines, few antivirals, diagnostic challenges, and rising spillover risk. Future research: expand reservoir surveillance, study environmental factors, and develop vaccines and antivirals.

papers.title_zh: 汉坦病毒 疾病:病毒学、临床特征和治疗方法综述

analysis_zh.background: 汉坦病毒es 是全球性的动物源性病原体,可导致欧亚地区的 肾综合征出血热 和美洲的 汉坦病毒肺综合征,促使进行全面综述。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 本综述涉及啮齿动物传播、免疫介导的血管渗漏发病机制、有限的治疗选择、诊断挑战以及环境变化对传播的影响。
analysis_zh.current_state: 疾病严重程度取决于病毒种类、宿主免疫反应和及时的医疗救治,免疫驱动的血管渗漏被确定为导致严重结局的主要因素。
analysis_zh.gaps: 目前尚无全球批准的疫苗,抗病毒药物稀缺,特定物种的诊断困难,且环境和城市化趋势加大了跨种传播风险。
analysis_zh.future_research: 重点包括对储存宿主的大规模生态监测、环境决定因素的研究,以及疫苗和靶向抗病毒药物的开发。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：汉坦病毒es 是全球性的动物源性病原体,可导致欧亚地区的 肾综合征出血热 和美洲的 汉坦病毒肺综合征,促使进行全面综述。 主要方向：本综述涉及啮齿动物传播、免疫介导的血管渗漏发病机制、有限的治疗选择、诊断挑战以及环境变化对传播的影响。 研究现状：疾病严重程度取决于病毒种类、宿主免疫反应和及时的医疗救治,免疫驱动的血管渗漏被确定为导致严重结局的主要因素。 不足：目前尚无全球批准的疫苗,抗病毒药物稀缺,特定物种的诊断困难,且环境和城市化趋势加大了跨种传播风险。 后续研究：重点包括对储存宿主的大规模生态监测、环境决定因素的研究,以及疫苗和靶向抗病毒药物的开发。

papers.title: Expedition cruises, island hopping, and zoonotic risk: governance and operational lessons from the MV Hondius Andes hantavirus outbreak
papers.abstract: The Andes orthohantavirus outbreak linked to the MV Hondius expedition cruise illustrates how a probable land-based zoonotic exposure can become a multinational public health event when it intersects with enclosed shipboard environments, delayed clinical recognition, remote navigation, medical evacuation, and international passenger dispersal. Although hantavirus infection is classically associated with exposure to infected rodents or contaminated environments, Andes virus is exceptional among orthohantaviruses because limited person-to-person transmission has been documented, particularly after close and prolonged contact. This Perspective uses the MV Hondius outbreak as an analytical case study to identify governance and operational gaps in expedition-era travel medicine. Existing International Health Regulations, WHO ship-event guidance, and ECDC recommendations provide essential foundations for coordination, notification, isolation, and contact tracing; however, this outbreak exposed expedition-specific gaps in safe port access, medical evacuation, onboard recognition of nonspecific febrile illness, diagnostic escalation, passenger traceability, and post-disembarkation monitoring. We therefore propose an accountability-oriented One Health preparedness model that operationalizes existing guidance through route-level risk assessment, exposure-history assessment, onboard syndromic surveillance, isolation and telemedicine triggers, reference-laboratory pathways, port-of-call agreements, auditable passenger and excursion records, and cross-border post-travel monitoring. The central lesson is not that expedition cruises, birdwatching, or ecological tourism are inherently unsafe, but that their expanding geographic reach requires binding, auditable, and expedition-specific outbreak protocols before passengers embark.

papers.full_text: Frontiers in Medicine 01 frontiersin.org TYPE Perspective PUBLISHED 16 July 2026 DOI 10.3389/fmed.2026.1892006 OPEN ACCESS EDITED BY Rajni Kant, Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), India REVIEWED BY Hala Abou El Naja, Senior Epidemiologist, Cairo, Egypt *CORRESPONDENCE Esteban Ortiz-Prado e.ortizprado@gmail.com RECEIVED 26 May 2026 REVISED 25 June 2026 ACCEPTED 30 June 2026 PUBLISHED 16 July 2026 CITATION ​Ortiz-Prado E, Cadena MP, ​ Vethencourt-Ysea MA, ​ Vasconez-Gonzalez J, Douglas KO and ​ Izquierdo-Condoy JS (2026) Expedition cruises, island hopping, and zoonotic risk: governance and operational lessons from the MV Hondius Andes hantavirus outbreak. Front. Med. 13:1892006. doi: 10.3389/fmed.2026.1892006 COPYRIGHT © 2026 Ortiz-Prado, Cadena, Vethencourt-Ysea, Vasconez-Gonzalez, Douglas and Izquierdo-Condoy. The use, distribution or reproduction in other forums is permitted, provided the original author(s) and the copyright owner(s) are credited and that the original publication in this journal is cited, in accordance with accepted academic practice. No use, distribution or reproduction is permitted which does not comply with these terms. Expedition cruises, island hopping, and zoonotic risk: governance and operational lessons from the MV Hondius Andes hantavirus outbreak Esteban Ortiz-Prado 1*, María Paz Cadena 1, María A. Vethencourt-Ysea 2, Jorge Vasconez-Gonzalez 1, Kirk O. Douglas 3 and Juan S. Izquierdo-Condoy 1 1One Health Research Group, Universidad de Las Americas, Quito, Ecuador, 2Universidad Latina de Costa Rica, San José, Costa Rica, 3Centre for Biosecurity Studies, The University of the West Indies, St. Michael, Barbados The Andes orthohantavirus outbreak linked to the MV Hondius expedition cruise illustrates how a probable land-based zoonotic exposure can become a mul­ tinational public health event when it intersects with enclosed shipboard envi­ ronments, delayed clinical recognition, remote navigation, medical evacuation, and international passenger dispersal. Although hantavirus infection is classically associated with exposure to infected rodents or contaminated environments, Andes virus is exceptional among orthohantaviruses because limited person- to-person transmission has been documented, particularly after close and pro­ longed contact. This Perspective uses the MV Hondius outbreak as an analytical case study to identify governance and operational gaps in expedition-era travel medicine. Existing International Health Regulations, WHO ship-event guidance, and ECDC recommendations provide essential foundations for coordination, notification, isolation, and contact tracing; however, this outbreak exposed expe­ dition-specific gaps in safe port access, medical evacuation, onboard recogni­ tion of nonspecific febrile illness, diagnostic escalation, passenger traceability, and post-disembarkation monitoring. We therefore propose an accountability- oriented One Health preparedness model that operationalizes existing guidance through route-level risk assessment, exposure-history assessment, onboard syn­ dromic surveillance, isolation and telemedicine triggers, reference-laboratory pathways, port-of-call agreements, auditable passenger and excursion records, and cross-border post-travel monitoring. The central lesson is not that expedi­ tion cruises, birdwatching, or ecological tourism are inherently unsafe, but that their expanding geographic reach requires binding, auditable, and expedition- specific outbreak protocols before passengers embark. KEYWORDS Andes virus, ecological tourism, expedition cruises, One Health, orthohantavirus, travel-associated infections Ortiz-Prado et al.  10.3389/fmed.2026.1892006 Frontiers in Medicine 02 frontiersin.org 1 Introduction The recent Andes orthohantavirus outbreak linked to the MV Hondius expedition cruise should be interpreted as more than an iso­ lated maritime health event. It represents a rare and highly instructive One Health case study in which ecological exposure, international tourism, wildlife-associated environments, enclosed transport sys­ tems, delayed diagnosis, intensive care needs, and multi-country public health coordination converged within a single epidemiological chain. The event illustrates how a pathogen usually embedded in regional rodent–environment interfaces can become internationally disruptive when introduced into high-mobility travel networks. On 2 May 2026, the World Health Organization (WHO) was noti­ fied of a cluster of severe acute respiratory illness aboard the Dutch- flagged expedition cruise ship MV Hondius (1). By 25 May 2026, the outbreak linked to the MV Hondius had increased to 13 reported cases and three deaths, corresponding to an observed case fatality ratio of 25% (2). No additional deaths had been reported since 2 May, but more than 600 contacts across 30 countries remained under public health monitoring. Spain reported the most recent additional case in a traveler who was evacuated from the cruise and is isolating at a hos­ pital in Madrid for hantavirus (3). Earlier official reports had docu­ mented six laboratory-confirmed Andes virus infections by PCR or sequencing and two probable cases; subsequent updates expanded the number of reported cases as additional infections were identified among repatriated passengers and crew. The vessel had departed Ushuaia, Argentina, on 1 April 2026, after several passengers had trav­ elled through areas of Argentina, Chile, and Uruguay before boarding (2). The initial WHO report described the probable index case as an adult male who had travelled through the Southern Cone for more than 3 months before embarkation, developed symptoms on 6 April, and died onboard on 11 April. His close contact and travel companion later deteriorated after disembarkation and died in Johannesburg on 26 April; she was subsequently confirmed by PCR as infected with hantavirus. Subsequent clinical and genomic investigations confirmed Andes virus infection in additional cases, with early sequencing closely matching previously reported Argentine sequences, supporting a likely zoonotic introduction before departure while leaving open the contribution of limited person-to-person transmission aboard the vessel (1). This distinction is important because Andes virus is the only orthohantavirus with documented person-to-person transmis­ sion, typically after close and prolonged contact (1, 4). Orthohantaviruses are zoonotic viruses maintained primarily in rodent reservoirs (5). Human infection usually occurs through inhala­ tion of aerosolized particles from rodent urine, feces, or saliva, par­ ticularly in contaminated enclosed spaces or heavily infested environments (6). Clinically, orthohantavirus infections are classically associated with two major syndromic patterns: hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome, mainly reported in Europe and Asia, and hanta­ virus cardiopulmonary syndrome, predominantly described in the Americas; nephropathia epidemica is generally considered a milder form of hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (7, 8). In the Americas, orthohantavirus infection can lead to hantavirus cardiopul­ monary syndrome, a severe illness characterized by an initial febrile or gastrointestinal phase followed, in some cases, by rapid cardiopul­ monary deterioration (9, 10). This Perspective uses the MV Hondius outbreak as an analytical case study to identify governance and operational gaps in outbreak preparedness for expedition cruises. Rather than proposing a framework that duplicates existing IHR 2005, WHO, or ECDC mechanisms, we ask whether these mechanisms are sufficiently operational for remote, high-mobility, expedition-specific contexts. The outbreak exposed two critical vulnerabilities: the absence of binding, pre-arranged protocols for safe port access and medical evacuation of vessels with suspected or confirmed high-conse­ quence infections, and the lack of a standardized passenger trace­ ability system capable of supporting rapid post-disembarkation monitoring across multiple jurisdictions. The central lesson is therefore not only that expedition cruises require One Health awareness, but that this awareness must be translated into enforce­ able, accountable, and auditable preparedness arrangements before departure. 2 Cruise ships as mobile mass-gathering platforms: enclosed, crowded, isolated and outbreak-prone Cruise ships are not merely transport platforms, they are tempo­ rary mobile communities involving passengers and crew who share cabins, dining areas, corridors, recreational spaces, medical facilities, ventilation systems, sanitation infrastructure, and disembarkation pathways (11, 12). Expedition cruises add further complexity as they deliberately move passengers into remote, wildlife diverse, and often under-surveilled environments where human–animal–environment interfaces are intense and public health infrastructure may be limited (13, 14). From an epidemiological perspective, cruise ships can combine several outbreak-promoting conditions. First, high-density human interaction in semi-enclosed spaces is facilitated. Second, a significant proportion of older passengers, many of whom may have comorbidi­ ties, increasing the risk of severe outcomes after infection are repeat cruise passengers (15). Third, their operations are often far from ter­ tiary hospitals, making diagnosis, isolation, evacuation, and access to intensive care logistically difficult (13). Fourth, they connect multiple jurisdictions within short periods, complicating case investigation, contact tracing, quarantine decisions, and risk communication (15, 16) (Table 1). Finally, due to passenger disembarkment at ports and subsequent repatriation through commercial or charter flights, a shipboard event can rapidly become a multi-country public health challenge. The MV Hondius event illustrates some of these population’s dynamics. The outbreak involved passengers and crew from multiple countries, required medical evacuation, laboratory testing across international networks, and coordination through the International Health Regulations. The long incubation period of Andes virus infection fur­ ther complicated public health management, because exposed indi­ viduals required monitoring for up to 42 days after their last potential exposure (17) (Figure 1). Importantly, the MV Hondius event should not be interpreted as evidence that the cruise ship itself was the ecological source of infec­ tion. Orthohantaviruses are primarily maintained in rodent reser­ voirs, and human infection usually follows environmental exposure to contaminated rodent excreta. In this case, the clinical timeline suggests that the probable index case was likely infected before boarding, most plausibly during travel in the Southern Cone of South Ortiz-Prado et al.  10.3389/fmed.2026.1892006 Frontiers in Medicine 03 frontiersin.org America, with symptom onset occurring only after the ship had departed (2). This distinction is essential: the central epidemiological question concerns the pre-boarding exposure history of the index case, particularly during the weeks before embarkation. Nevertheless, once illness developed onboard, the cruise ship became a public health amplifier of complexity. Close quarters, shared spaces, delayed clinical recognition, limited onboard diagnostic capacity, and inter­ national passenger movement transformed a likely land-based zoo­ notic exposure into a multinational surveillance, communication, and response challenge. Thus, the risk posed by cruise ships in such events lies not in serving as the natural reservoir, but in their capacity to complicate detection, containment, contact tracing, and cross- border coordination. This epidemiological context also defines the limits of pre-board­ ing screening. If the probable index case acquired infection before embarkation and was still within the incubation period at the time of boarding, symptom-based screening alone would not have detected the risk. Therefore, the main operational lesson is not that pre-boarding screening should be treated as the primary safeguard, but that expedition cruises require layered detection and escalation systems after departure. These include structured exposure-history assessment, early onboard recognition of nonspecific febrile or gas­ trointestinal illness, immediate isolation triggers, differential diagno­ sis algorithms for severe travel-associated infections, telemedicine consultation, and pre-arranged diagnostic referral pathways. For long-incubation zoonoses, preparedness must extend beyond embar­ kation and continue throughout the voyage and after disembarkation. 3 The global scale of cruise tourism The global cruise industry has reached an unprecedented scale, creating important One Health challenges for zoonotic dis­ ease emergence, detection, and dissemination. By the end of 2025, the worldwide ocean cruise fleet was estimated at approximately 370 ships, with a total passenger capacity of about 704,200 berths (18). Major operators dominate this capacity: Carnival Corporation accounted for approximately 95 ships and 257,120 berths; Royal Caribbean Group for 58 ships and 155,430 berths; and Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings, including Oceania Cruises and Regent Seven Seas Cruises, for 34 ships and 71,510 berths. The remaining 183 vessels, including luxury, niche, and expedi­ tion operators, represented approximately 220,120 berths (Figure 2). This scale is epidemiologically relevant. The Cruise Lines International Association reported that global ocean cruise passen­ ger volume reached a historic high of 37. million passengers in 2025 (19), while independent industry estimates projected more than 38 million passengers in 2026 (20). Although mass-market vessels account for most global capacity, the expedition cruise segment including small, specialized ships such as the MV Hondius, with a capacity of approximately 170–196 passengers is expanding in response to demand for immersive nature-based travel. These vessels routinely perform island-hopping itineraries through remote and ecologically sensitive regions, including Patagonia, Antarctica, South Georgia, the South Atlantic islands, and other biodiversity-rich des­ tinations. With continued fleet growth and diversification of cruise itineraries, the frequency of human–animal–environment interfaces in zoonotic hotspots is likely to increase, amplifying the public health implications illustrated by the MV Hondius Andes virus outbreak. 4 Ecological tourism, island hopping and overlooked exposure interfaces Ecological tourism is often framed as low-impact because it is educational, conservation-oriented, or scientifically motivated (21). However, the MV Hondius outbreak underscores that some forms of ecotourism can place travelers in contact with underrecognized zoo­ notic interfaces. Birdwatchers, wildlife photographers, naturalists, expedition guides, and adventure tourists may visit wetlands, forest edges, rural roads, islands, docks, farms, abandoned structures, TABLE 1 The factors, epidemiological relevance and One Health implication of how expedition cruises can amplify infectious disease risk. Cruise- related factor Epidemiological relevance One Health implication High passenger density Frequent close contact in shared indoor spaces Increased risk of transmission for pathogens capable of human-to-human spread Enclosed environments Cabins, dining rooms, lecture halls, corridors, and medical rooms may concentrate exposure Need for ventilation, isolation protocols, and infection prevention measures Older passenger demographics Many expedition cruises attract older adults, sometimes with comorbidities Higher risk of severe disease, hospitalization, and intensive care need Remote navigation Ships may be days away from tertiary hospitals Requires pre-planned evacuation and telemedicine pathways Multi-country itineraries Passengers may disembark in several jurisdictions Requires international contact tracing and IHR coordination Delayed symptom recognition Early hantavirus symptoms may resemble influenza-like or gastrointestinal illness Need for shipboard syndromic surveillance Long incubation period Andes virus symptoms may appear weeks after exposure Requires post-travel monitoring and risk-stratified follow- up Limited onboard diagnostics Ship medical units usually lack advanced pathogen testing Need for rapid molecular platforms and reference- laboratory networks Ortiz-Prado et al.  10.3389/fmed.2026.1892006 Frontiers in Medicine 04 frontiersin.org storage areas, rustic cabins, food markets, landfills, and waste-dis­ posal sites (22–24). These settings are transition zones where humans, wildlife, synanthropic animals, rodents, food waste, con­ taminated dust, and environmental pathogens may converge (Figure 3). In the current Andes hantavirus cruise outbreak, the probable index case was described in official reports as an older adult trav­ eler who had spent several months in the Southern Cone before embarkation and developed symptoms after departure (1). The individual is not named here because personal identification does not add to the epidemiological assessment of the outbreak and is not necessary for public health interpretation. Although media reports have discussed possible exposure sites, no definitive source of infection has been confirmed. Therefore, this outbreak should be interpreted cautiously. Its value lies in illustrating how expedition cruises and ecological tourism may create opportuni­ ties for zoonotic exposure and subsequent outbreak amplification in enclosed travel settings, but the available evidence does not confirm a specific source, site of exposure, or transmission path­ way. The landfill hypothesis should not be presented as a con­ firmed source of infection; rather, it is best und…

full_text_sections.full_text: Frontiers in Medicine 01 frontiersin.org TYPE Perspective PUBLISHED 16 July 2026 DOI 10.3389/fmed.2026.1892006 OPEN ACCESS EDITED BY Rajni Kant, Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), India REVIEWED BY Hala Abou El Naja, Senior Epidemiologist, Cairo, Egypt *CORRESPONDENCE Esteban Ortiz-Prado e.ortizprado@gmail.com RECEIVED 26 May 2026 REVISED 25 June 2026 ACCEPTED 30 June 2026 PUBLISHED 16 July 2026 CITATION ​Ortiz-Prado E, Cadena MP, ​ Vethencourt-Ysea MA, ​ Vasconez-Gonzalez J, Douglas KO and ​ Izquierdo-Condoy JS (2026) Expedition cruises, island hopping, and zoonotic risk: governance and operational lessons from the MV Hondius Andes hantavirus outbreak. Front. Med. 13:1892006. doi: 10.3389/fmed.2026.1892006 COPYRIGHT © 2026 Ortiz-Prado, Cadena, Vethencourt-Ysea, Vasconez-Gonzalez, Douglas and Izquierdo-Condoy. This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License (CC BY). The use, distribution or reproduction in other forums is permitted, provided the original author(s) and the copyright owner(s) are credited and that the original publication in this journal is cited, in accordance with accepted academic practice. No use, distribution or reproduction is permitted which does not comply with these terms. Expedition cruises, island hopping, and zoonotic risk: governance and operational lessons from the MV Hondius Andes hantavirus outbreak Esteban Ortiz-Prado 1*, María Paz Cadena 1, María A. Vethencourt-Ysea 2, Jorge Vasconez-Gonzalez 1, Kirk O. Douglas 3 and Juan S. Izquierdo-Condoy 1 1One Health Research Group, Universidad de Las Americas, Quito, Ecuador, 2Universidad Latina de Costa Rica, San José, Costa Rica, 3Centre for Biosecurity Studies, The University of the West Indies, St. Michael, Barbados The Andes orthohantavirus outbreak linked to the MV Hondius expedition cruise illustrates how a probable land-based zoonotic exposure can become a mul­ tinational public health event when it intersects with enclosed shipboard envi­ ronments, delayed clinical recognition, remote navigation, medical evacuation, and international passenger dispersal. Although hantavirus infection is classically associated with exposure to infected rodents or contaminated environments, Andes virus is exceptional among orthohantaviruses because limited person- to-person transmission has been documented, particularly after close and pro­ longed contact. This Perspective uses the MV Hondius outbreak as an analytical case study to identify governance and operational gaps in expedition-era travel medicine. Existing International Health Regulations, WHO ship-event guidance, and ECDC recommendations provide essential foundations for coordination, notification, isolation, and contact tracing; however, this outbreak exposed expe­ dition-specific gaps in safe port access, medical evacuation, onboard recogni­ tion of nonspecific febrile illness, diagnostic escalation, passenger traceability, and post-disembarkation monitoring. We therefore propose an accountability- oriented One Health preparedness model that operationalizes existing guidance through route-level risk assessment, exposure-history assessment, onboard syn­ dromic surveillance, isolation and telemedicine triggers, reference-laboratory pathways, port-of-call agreements, auditable passenger and excursion records, and cross-border post-travel monitoring. The central lesson is not that expedi­ tion cruises, birdwatching, or ecological tourism are inherently unsafe, but that their expanding geographic reach requires binding, auditable, and expedition- specific outbreak protocols before passengers embark. KEYWORDS Andes virus, ecological tourism, expedition cruises, One Health, orthohantavirus, travel-associated infections Ortiz-Prado et al.  10.3389/fmed.2026.1892006 Frontiers in Medicine 02 frontiersin.org 1 Introduction The recent Andes orthohantavirus outbreak linked to the MV Hondius expedition cruise should be interpreted as more than an iso­ lated maritime health event. It represents a rare and highly instructive One Health case study in which ecological exposure, international tourism, wildlife-associated environments, enclosed transport sys­ tems, delayed diagnosis, intensive care needs, and multi-country public health coordination converged within a single epidemiological chain. The event illustrates how a pathogen usually embedded in regional rodent–environment interfaces can become internationally disruptive when introduced into high-mobility travel networks. On 2 May 2026, the World Health Organization (WHO) was noti­ fied of a cluster of severe acute respiratory illness aboard the Dutch- flagged expedition cruise ship MV Hondius (1). By 25 May 2026, the outbreak linked to the MV Hondius had increased to 13 reported cases and three deaths, corresponding to an observed case fatality ratio of 25% (2). No additional deaths had been reported since 2 May, but more than 600 contacts across 30 countries remained under public health monitoring. Spain reported the most recent additional case in a traveler who was evacuated from the cruise and is isolating at a hos­ pital in Madrid for hantavirus (3). Earlier official reports had docu­ mented six laboratory-confirmed Andes virus infections by PCR or sequencing and two probable cases; subsequent updates expanded the number of reported cases as additional infections were identified among repatriated passengers and crew. The vessel had departed Ushuaia, Argentina, on 1 April 2026, after several passengers had trav­ elled through areas of Argentina, Chile, and Uruguay before boarding (2). The initial WHO report described the probable index case as an adult male who had travelled through the Southern Cone for more than 3 months before embarkation, developed symptoms on 6 April, and died onboard on 11 April. His close contact and travel companion later deteriorated after disembarkation and died in Johannesburg on 26 April; she was subsequently confirmed by PCR as infected with hantavirus. Subsequent clinical and genomic investigations confirmed Andes virus infection in additional cases, with early sequencing closely matching previously reported Argentine sequences, supporting a likely zoonotic introduction before departure while leaving open the contribution of limited person-to-person transmission aboard the vessel (1). This distinction is important because Andes virus is the only orthohantavirus with documented person-to-person transmis­ sion, typically after close and prolonged contact (1, 4). Orthohantaviruses are zoonotic viruses maintained primarily in rodent reservoirs (5). Human infection usually occurs through inhala­ tion of aerosolized particles from rodent urine, feces, or saliva, par­ ticularly in contaminated enclosed spaces or heavily infested environments (6). Clinically, orthohantavirus infections are classically associated with two major syndromic patterns: hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome, mainly reported in Europe and Asia, and hanta­ virus cardiopulmonary syndrome, predominantly described in the Americas; nephropathia epidemica is generally considered a milder form of hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (7, 8). In the Americas, orthohantavirus infection can lead to hantavirus cardiopul­ monary syndrome, a severe illness characterized by an initial febrile or gastrointestinal phase followed, in some cases, by rapid cardiopul­ monary deterioration (9, 10). This Perspective uses the MV Hondius outbreak as an analytical case study to identify governance and operational gaps in outbreak preparedness for expedition cruises. Rather than proposing a framework that duplicates existing IHR 2005, WHO, or ECDC mechanisms, we ask whether these mechanisms are sufficiently operational for remote, high-mobility, expedition-specific contexts. The outbreak exposed two critical vulnerabilities: the absence of binding, pre-arranged protocols for safe port access and medical evacuation of vessels with suspected or conf…

analysis.background: The Andes orthohantavirus outbreak on the MV Hondius expedition cruise illustrates how a probable land‑based zoonotic exposure can become a multinational public‑health event when it intersects with enclosed shipboard environments, delayed clinical recognition, remote navigation, medical evacuation, and international passenger dispersal.
analysis.methods: The authors conducted a Perspective case‑study analysis of the MV Hondius outbreak to identify governance and operational gaps in expedition‑era travel medicine.
analysis.results: Existing International Health Regulations, WHO ship‑event guidance, and ECDC recommendations provide essential foundations for coordination, notification, isolation and contact tracing; however the outbreak exposed expedition‑specific gaps in safe port access, medical evacuation, onboard recognition of nonspecific febrile illness, diagnostic escalation, passenger traceability, and post‑disembarkation monitoring; the authors propose an accountability‑oriented One Health preparedness model that operationalizes existing guidance through route‑level risk assessment, exposure‑history assessment, onboard syndromic surveillance, isolation/telemedicine triggers, reference‑laboratory pathways, port‑of‑call agreements, auditable passenger and excursion records, and cross‑border post‑travel monitoring.
analysis.contribution: The paper contributes a One Health governance framework and operational recommendations to improve outbreak preparedness and response for ecological tourism and expedition cruises.
analysis.limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

analysis.summary_en: Background: The Andes orthohantavirus outbreak on the MV Hondius expedition cruise shows how a land‑based zoonotic exposure can become a multinational event via shipboard conditions and delayed recognition. Methods: The authors used a Perspective case‑study analysis of the outbreak to pinpoint governance and operational gaps. Results: While existing IHR, WHO, and ECDC guidance offers coordination foundations, the event revealed gaps in port access, medical evacuation, onboard illness recognition, diagnostics, passenger traceability, and post‑disembarkation monitoring; an accountability‑oriented One Health preparedness model is proposed. Contribution: The article provides a One Health framework and practical recommendations for improving outbreak preparedness in ecological tourism and expedition cruises. Limitations: Not reported in the supplied evidence.

papers.title_zh: 探险游轮、跳岛游和人畜共患病风险:MV Hondius Andes 汉坦病毒 疫情的治理和运营经验教训

analysis_zh.background: MV Hondius 探险游轮上的安第斯山脉 正汉坦病毒 疫情表明,当与封闭的船上环境、延迟的临床识别、远程导航、医疗疏散和国际乘客疏散相交叉时,可能的陆基人畜共患病暴露如何可能成为跨国公共卫生事件。
analysis_zh.methods: 作者对MV Hondius号疫情进行了一项视角案例研究分析,以识别探险时代旅行医学中的治理和运营缺口。
analysis_zh.results: 现行《国际卫生条例》、世卫组织船舶事件指南以及欧洲疾病预防控制中心的建议为协调、通报、隔离和接触者追踪提供了基本框架;然而本次疫情暴露了探险特有的安全港口进入、医疗撤离、船上非特异性发热疾病识别、诊断升级、乘客可追溯性以及下船后监测等缺口;作者提出了一种以问责为导向的“一体健康”应急准备模型,通过航线风险评估、暴露史评估、船上综合症监测、隔离/远程医疗触发、参考实验室通路、停靠港协议、可审计的乘客及岸上活动记录以及跨境旅行后监测,将现有指南落地实施。
analysis_zh.contribution: 本文提供了一套“一体健康”治理框架和运营建议,以提升生态旅游和探险邮轮的疫情防备与响应能力。
analysis_zh.limitations: 在提供的证据中未报告。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：MV Hondius 探险游轮上的安第斯山脉 正汉坦病毒 疫情表明,当与封闭的船上环境、延迟的临床识别、远程导航、医疗疏散和国际乘客疏散相交叉… 方法：作者对MV Hondius号疫情进行了一项视角案例研究分析,以识别探险时代旅行医学中的治理和运营缺口。 结果：现行《国际卫生条例》、世卫组织船舶事件指南以及欧洲疾病预防控制中心的建议为协调、通报、隔离和接触者追踪提供了基本框架;然而本次疫情暴露了探险特… 贡献：本文提供了一套“一体健康”治理框架和运营建议,以提升生态旅游和探险邮轮的疫情防备与响应能力。 局限：在提供的证据中未报告。

papers.title: Ribavirin post-exposure prophylaxis for Andes virus exposure: a viewpoint.
papers.abstract: Andes virus (ANDV) is unique among hantaviruses because person-to-person transmission is possible. This raises questions on the relevance of post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP), particularly following high-risk household, healthcare-associated, or laboratory exposures, considering its high case fatality rate. Ribavirin has demonstrated antiviral activity against hantaviruses in vitro and in animal models, although clinical evidence supporting its use for ANDV remains extremely limited. This viewpoint summarises the currently available evidence regarding ribavirin as PEP after potential ANDV exposure. We discuss the knowledge gaps that may limit the applicability of ribavirin PEP, to make informed decisions on the use of ribavirin in the setting of PEP. Potential dosing strategies are visualised by modelling and simulation, and potential dose and duration are discussed. Although the biological rationale for ribavirin PEP appears compelling, absence of controlled human studies and potential toxicity currently limit its role to highly selected exposure scenarios and use shortly after exposure.

analysis.background: Andes virus (ANDV) uniquely transmits person‑to‑person, raising the need for post‑exposure prophylaxis (PEP).
analysis.main_directions: PEP relevance for high‑risk household, healthcare‑associated, or laboratory exposures is questioned due to ANDV’s high fatality.
analysis.current_state: Ribavirin shows in‑vitro and animal antiviral activity, but clinical evidence for ANDV PEP is extremely limited.
analysis.gaps: Knowledge gaps, including lack of controlled human studies and potential toxicity, restrict ribavirin PEP to highly selected scenarios.
analysis.future_research: Modelling and simulation of dosing strategies highlight the need for clinical trials to define optimal dose and duration.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Andes virus (ANDV) uniquely transmits person‑to‑person, raising the need for post‑exposure prophylaxis (PEP). Main directions: PEP relevance for high‑risk household, healthcare‑associated, or laboratory exposures is questioned due to ANDV’s high fatality. Current state: Ribavirin shows in‑vitro and animal antiviral activity, but clinical evidence for ANDV PEP is extremely limited. Gaps: Knowledge gaps, including lack of controlled human studies and potential toxicity, restrict ribavirin PEP to highly selected scenarios. Future research: Modelling and simulation of dosing strategies highlight the need for clinical trials to define optimal dose and duration.

papers.title_zh: 利巴韦林暴露后预防[ PDI0008 ]: 观点

analysis_zh.background: 安第斯病毒(ANDV)唯一能够人际传播,提升了对暴露后预防(PEP)的需求。
analysis_zh.main_directions: 由于ANDV的高致死率,对高危家庭、医疗相关或实验室暴露的PEP相关性提出质疑。
analysis_zh.current_state: 利巴韦林在体外和动物实验中显示抗病毒活性,但针对安第斯病毒的临床PEP证据极为有限。
analysis_zh.gaps: 知识空白,包括缺乏受控的人体研究和潜在毒性,限制了利巴韦林PEP仅在高度挑选的情境中使用。
analysis_zh.future_research: 剂量策略的建模与模拟凸显了开展临床试验以确定最佳剂量和疗程的必要性。

papers.summary_zh: 背景：安第斯病毒(ANDV)唯一能够人际传播,提升了对暴露后预防(PEP)的需求。 主要方向：由于ANDV的高致死率,对高危家庭、医疗相关或实验室暴露的PEP相关性提出质疑。 研究现状：利巴韦林在体外和动物实验中显示抗病毒活性,但针对安第斯病毒的临床PEP证据极为有限。 不足：知识空白,包括缺乏受控的人体研究和潜在毒性,限制了利巴韦林PEP仅在高度挑选的情境中使用。 后续研究：剂量策略的建模与模拟凸显了开展临床试验以确定最佳剂量和疗程的必要性。

papers.title: Modeling the Transmission Dynamics of Hantavirus with Environmental Viral Persistence
papers.abstract: Hantavirus is a zoonotic infection maintained principally in rodent reservoirs, and human exposure occurs mainly through environments contaminated with infectious viral particles. This study formulates and analyses a deterministic compartmental model for hantavirus transmission involving susceptible and infected rodents, susceptible, exposed, infectious and recovered humans, and an environmental viral reservoir. The model includes logistic rodent growth, direct rodent-to-rodent transmission, indirect environmental transmission, viral shedding from infected classes and natural viral clearance. Fundamental qualitative properties of the system are established, including existence, uniqueness, positivity and boundedness of solutions within a feasible region. The disease-free equilibrium is obtained, and the basic reproduction number is derived using the next-generation matrix method. Stability analysis shows that the disease-free equilibrium is locally and globally asymptotically stable when R0 < 1, whereas disease persistence is associated with R0 > 1 and the existence of a biologically feasible endemic equilibrium. Local stability conditions for the endemic equilibrium are characterised through the Jacobian matrix and the Routh-Hurwitz criterion. The analysis indicates that environmental viral persistence provides an indirect pathway that can sustain transmission between infected rodents, susceptible rodents and humans. These findings suggest that reducing rodent infection, limiting environmental contamination and increasing viral clearance may help move the system below the epidemic threshold. The model offers a mathematically tractable framework for interpreting hantavirus persistence and for guiding future extensions involving seasonality, spatial structure, stochasticity and data-driven parameterisation.

analysis.background: Hantavirus is a zoonotic infection maintained principally in rodent reservoirs, with human exposure mainly through environments contaminated with infectious viral particles.
analysis.methods: The study formulates and analyses a deterministic compartmental model that includes susceptible and infected rodents, susceptible, exposed, infectious and recovered humans, and an environmental viral reservoir, incorporating logistic rodent growth, direct and indirect transmission, viral shedding, and natural viral clearance.
analysis.results: The model’s qualitative properties (existence, uniqueness, positivity, boundedness) are established; the disease‑free equilibrium and basic reproduction number (R0) are derived; stability analysis shows the disease‑free equilibrium is locally and globally asymptotically stable when R0 < 1 and persistence occurs when R0 > 1 with an endemic equilibrium; environmental viral persistence provides an indirect pathway sustaining transmission; reducing rodent infection, limiting environmental contamination, and increasing viral clearance may lower R0 below the epidemic threshold.
analysis.contribution: The work provides a mathematically tractable framework for interpreting hantavirus persistence and informs future extensions such as seasonality, spatial structure, stochasticity, and data‑driven parameterisation.
analysis.limitations: Only an abstract is provided; no empirical data, parameter estimates, or validation are reported.

analysis.summary_en: Background: Hantavirus is a rodent‑borne zoonosis transmitted to humans via contaminated environments. Methods: A deterministic compartmental model with rodent, human, and environmental compartments was formulated, including logistic rodent growth, direct and indirect transmission, shedding, and clearance. Results: The model shows existence, uniqueness, positivity, and boundedness; derives R0 and equilibrium states; finds the disease‑free equilibrium stable when R0<1 and endemic persistence when R0>1; highlights environmental persistence as a key indirect transmission route; suggests control via reducing rodent infection, environmental contamination, and enhancing viral clearance. Contribution: Offers a tractable mathematical framework for understanding hantavirus persistence and guiding future model extensions. Limitations: Only an abstract is available; no empirical data or validation are reported.

papers.title_zh: 利用环境病毒持久性对 汉坦病毒 的传播动力学进行建模

analysis_zh.background: 汉坦病毒是一种主要在啮齿动物宿主中维持的人畜共患感染,人类暴露主要通过被传染性病毒颗粒污染的环境。
analysis_zh.methods: 本研究构建并分析了一个确定性分区模型,包含易感和感染的啮齿动物,易感、暴露、感染和康复的人群,以及环境病毒库,模型纳入了啮齿动物的逻辑增长、直接和间接传播、病毒脱落以及自然病毒清除。
analysis_zh.results: 建立了模型的定性性质(存在性、唯一性、正性、有界性);推导出无病平衡点和基本再生数 (R0);稳定性分析表明,当 R0 < 1 时,无病平衡点局部和全局渐近稳定;当 R0 > 1 时出现持续流行并存在地方性平衡;环境病毒的持续存在提供了维持传播的间接途径;降低啮齿动物感染、限制环境污染、提升病毒清除可将 R0 降低至流行阈值以下。
analysis_zh.contribution: 该工作提供了一个数学上可处理的框架用于解释 汉坦病毒 的持续性,并为未来的扩展提供参考,如季节性、空间结构、随机性以及基于数据的参数化。
analysis_zh.limitations: 仅提供了摘要;未报告实证数据、参数估计或模型验证。

papers.summary_zh: 背景:汉坦病毒是一种主要在啮齿动物宿主中维持的人畜共患感染,人类暴露主要通过被传染性病毒颗粒污染的环... 方法:本研究构建并分析了一个确定性分区模型,包含易感和感染的啮齿动物,易感、暴露、感染和康复的人群,... 结果:建立了模型的定性性质(存在性、唯一性、正性、有界性);推导出无病平衡点和基本再生数 (R0);... 贡献:该工作提供了一个数学上可处理的框架用于解释 汉坦病毒 的持续性,并为未来的扩展提供参考,如季节... 局限:仅提供了摘要;未报告实证数据、参数估计或模型验证。

papers.title: Enfermedades transmitidas por vectores: chagas, dengue, malaria y hantavirus
papers.abstract: Rol estratégico de la Atención Primaria: el personal médico de familia es fundamental para el cribado inicial, el manejo ambulatorio de los casos leves y la prevención del colapso hospitalario. Alta sospecha clínica: el diagnóstico inicial se fundamenta en vincular un síndrome febril inespecífico con un nexo epidemiológico claro (viajes recientes, picaduras o exposición a roedores/brotes cerrados). Identificación de «banderas rojas»: es prioritario reconocer los signos de alarma (como sangrado de mucosas, dolor abdominal intenso, compromiso respiratorio o descompensación cardíaca) para garantizar una derivación hospitalaria inmediata y oportuna. Acción comunitaria: la educación para el control del vector y la notificación epidemiológica son pilares indelegables en el abordaje integral desde el centro de salud.

papers.title: Hantavirus and Middle East Public Health Challenges: A Subject Review
papers.abstract: Hantaviruses are emerging zoonotic viruses belonging to the family Hantaviridae, which are typically found in rodent reservoirs and transmitted to humans through contact with contaminated saliva, aerosolized particles or excreta. There are two major clinical syndromes associated with human infections: hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) — mostly reported from Europe and Asia, and hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS) — mostly reported from the Americas. The Middle East has a relatively low incidence of hantavirus disease, but recent epidemiological studies have shown that the virus is circulating in several countries, including Türkiye and Iran. The emergence of hantavirus infections in this area has become a growing concern as the population increases, climate variations occur, the rodents' ecology is changing, the agriculture spreads and the surveillance of this situation is improving. There is limited surveillance, underdiagnosis, and a large clinical overlap with other febrile illnesses all of which are important challenges in quantifying the regional burden of disease. This review aims at summarizing the state of knowledge about the epidemiology of the hantavirus in the Middle East, focusing on geographical distribution, environmental factors, rodent hosts, epidemiology, clinical features, diagnostic methods and public health preparedness. Current data suggest that while human infections are rare, ecological conditions which could sustain hantavirus are present in several Middle Eastern countries, and therefore increased surveillance and research into interdisciplinary and One Health initiatives are needed. Creating closer ties between the medical, veterinary and environmental communities is crucial for early outbreak detection and prevention in the future.
papers.journal: European Journal of Ecology, Biology and Agriculture
